titleStmt.title: Letter from Roger Casement to Dr. Theobald von Bethmann Hollweg, Imperial Chancellor of Germany, 11 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Roger Casement

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This letter is from Roger Casement (1864-1916) to the German Imperial Chancellor, Theobald von Bethmann Hollweg. Roger Casement, born in Sandycove was a former British Consul who, disillusioned with British imperialism became an Irish Nationalist. On his return to Ireland from Germany on Good Friday 1916 he was arrested at Banna Strand. He was tried for high treason and hanged at Pentonville Prison on 3 August 1916. In this letter Roger Casement writes to Theobold von Bethmann Hollweg looking for assurance that the members of the Irish Brigade in Germany will not be mistreated after his departure to Ireland. He makes reference to an interview the two men had on 18 December 1914 with regards to German Policy towards Ireland and an agreement dating from 23-28 December that the volunteers of the Irish Brigade, now at Zossen, would not be harmed. The Irish Brigade was mad up of Irish soldiers who had served in the British army but were captured by the German Imperial army and were now prisoners of war. Casement suggested that these men would form an Irish Brigade and go to Ireland to fight against the British army there. However, there was very little uptake among the Irish prisoners of war, only 53 as Casement mentions in the letter. Casement does not agree with the German Government that these low numbers have rendered the agreement guaranteeing the safety of the Irish Brigade null in void, and reminds the chancellor of the terms of the agreement. He trusts that the von Bethmann Hollweg will honour the terms of the agreement.

msIdentifier.collection: Roger Casement Papers, Collection List No A15; Ms. 13,085 /9

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Berlin 11th April 1916. Excellency, I venture On the point
of leaving Germany on a mission the
voice definitely probably cut me off from
all further communication with
this country — at least during the
course of the war — I venture to
bring to you Excellency's notice
the situation of the small body
of Irish soldiers, who volunteered
to form an Irish brigade corps to fight
for Irish freedom with the whole—
hearted support and approval of
the Imperial German Govt. The terms on which they agreed
to serve andton the observancy which the Imperial
Govt. pledged its good faith in
the most formal way are embodied
in the agreement drawn up by
myself on 23 Decr 1914 and accepted
by the Under Secretary of State — Here
on 28 Decr 1914. I need not refer your Excellency to that
Document, since it was so fully
within your Excellency's cognizance
following the interview on the
subject of German policy toward
Ireland I had the honour to have
with your Ex. on 18th Decr.
1914. Now that I am leaving
Germany, as I hope for Ireland
in order to help us the best
in my power in the very
critical circumstances in
which events (over which I have
had no control) seem to push
my country, I cannot depart
without recalling the pledges bound
of the Imperial German Govt.
situation of to these Irish soldiers who might
volunteered for the cause of
Ireland under the pledge of that agreement and begging that
nothing should be allowed to
interfere with their it strict
fulfilment in their regard.
These men remain, in honour
a charge of honour confided
to the honour of the German Govt. and
German people. They are guests of
Germany in the highest sense
of the word and are entitled to
be treated with the scrupulous fulfilment regard
for the stipulations terms of the Agreement
under which they threw off their
allegiance to the and entered into
rebellion. The German Govt. was
a full consulting party to that
act of their and did all in
its power through its military
chiefs, to assure these men
that their "treason" was honourable
in itself, since it was for their
oppressed country, and assured them that they
should be most honourably treated
during the period of their stay
on German soil. Today these military chiefs
through the channel of the Political
Section of the Gr. General Staff,
assure me I am informed that they regard the
agreement of 23-28 Decr. 1914 is
null and void and having has no binding
force on their salary in relation to the men. That the Irish
soldiers now at Zossen, to this
number of some 53 4 (a
list of which I append) are
"Deserters", or "prisoners of war"
and may be treated in any way
that seems easiest for fitting forto the military
authorities. Dissenting profoundly from this point
of view, I am forced to refer
the treatment of these Irishmen soldiers volunteers
to your Excellency as the highest
representative of the Imperial
Gr. Govt. whose good faith is
then called in question by a
main Department of one
of the Depts of State, but
not doubted by family,
and to beg that in the firm
conviction that every stipulation
of the agreement of 23—28 Decr. 1914
in so far as it affects the
men, their status here in Germany
and their ultimate treatment disposal
on the conclusion peace will
be faithfully fulfilled by
the highest authorities of
the Country and Government and or this great
country.
To Chancellor 11 April 1916 I have requested my friend
Mr.T.St John Gaffney, former
U.S Consul at Munich to have
a kindly interest in the
men so far as this may be
possible and am recommending
him in this capacity to
Count Georg von Wedel of the
German Foreign Office whose
assurances to me on behalf
of the men I recall with
gratitude and confidence. I have the honour to be,
Excellency,
Your Excellency's
most obedient
humble servant, Roger Casement His Excellency,
Dr. von Bethmann Hollweg
Imperial Chancellor Berlin
